beats.prompt: Quick warm-up from L4 — match each term to what it does in an N-hypothesis update.

pairs.right: rescale each by its likelihood, then renormalize
how well a hypothesis predicted the evidence

feedback.correct: Three hypotheses, same rule: rescale each by its likelihood, then renormalize. The doors will obey it.
hints: From L4: the N-way update is the 2-way rule with more terms.
Likelihood = how well each hypothesis predicted what you saw.
Rescale each of the three doors by its likelihood, then divide by the total.

beats.prompt: Three doors: a car behind one, goats behind the others. You pick Door 1. The host (who knows) opens Door 3 to reveal a goat and offers you the switch to Door 2. Stay or switch — or doesn't it matter?

options: Doesn't matter — two doors left, so 50/50

Stay — switching is just superstition.note: Your door was fixed at 1/3 before the host acted. Switching captures the leftover 2/3.

Doesn't matter — two doors left, so 50/5.note: The two doors aren't symmetric. Your door kept its original 1/3; all the leftover 2/3 funnels onto the single door the host chose to leave closed.

Switch — it wins more often.note: Right — switching wins 2/3 of the time. Let's see why the host's forced hand is the evidence.

hints: No wrong guess — we'll run the 3-door update.
Two doors left does NOT mean 50/50 — they aren't symmetric.
Your door stays 1/3; the leftover 2/3 funnels to the switch door.

beats.prompt: Read this just-in-time primer on the host's protocol — then continue.

interaction.title: The protocol sets the likelihood
interaction.body: The host isn't a coin — he follows rules. The likelihood of 'he opened Door 3' depends on those rules: when the car is behind the other live door he's forced to open Door 3 (likelihood 1); when it's behind your door he opens Door 3 only half the time (likelihood ½). The asymmetry between 1 and ½ is the evidence.

feedback.correct: The protocol — the host's rules — sets the likelihood of his action. That's where the evidence lives.
hints: The data isn't 'a goat appeared'; it's which door he opened.
His rules constrain him: forced to open Door 3 (L=1) vs free to (L=½).
Likelihood of the action comes from the protocol, not from chance.

beats.prompt: Watch the reveal collapse the opened door to 0 and funnel its probability onto the door the host left closed — switch = 2/3.

hero.structuralReadout: Your door was fixed at 1/3; the host's forced hand funnels the other 2/3 onto the switch door, leaving the opened door at 0 → 2/3.

feedback.correct: The host's reveal sends the opened door to 0 and funnels its probability onto the door he left closed: switch = 2/3, your door = 1/3, opened = 0.
hints: Watch all three start at 1/3, then the opened door collapses to 0.
Your door can't gain from a reveal it didn't cause — it stays 1/3.
All the freed-up probability lands on the one door the host avoided: 2/3.

beats.prompt: For each door, give the probability that the host opens Door 3 — given where the car is.

fields.label: P(host opens Door 3 | car behind Door 1)
P(host opens Door 3 | car behind Door 2)
P(host opens Door 3 | car behind Door 3)

feedback.correct: P(opens 3 | car behind 1) = 1/2 (free to open 2 or 3); P(opens 3 | car behind 2) = 1 (forced — can't open your door or the car); P(opens 3 | car behind 3) = 0 (never reveals the car). That 1 vs ½ is the whole edge.
hints: Ask: given where the car is, can the host still open Door 3?
Car behind your door → he has a free choice (½). Car behind Door 2 → he's forced to open 3 (1). Car behind Door 3 → impossible (0).

beats.prompt: Each door's prior is 1/3. Weighted by the likelihoods [½, 1, 0] and renormalized — what is P(car behind Door 2 | host opened Door 3)?

fields.label: P(car behind Door 2 | host opened Door 3)

beats.interviewNote: Monty Hall — treating the host's action as evidence whose likelihood comes from his protocol — is a canonical Bayes interview question (interview pack `showcase-monty-hall`). The clean framing: your door is fixed at prior odds 1:2 against the car; the host's forced hand transfers the entire 2/3 onto the one door he avoided.

feedback.correct: Each door's prior is 1/3. Weighted by [½, 1, 0] and renormalized: (1/3·1)/(1/3·½ + 1/3·1 + 1/3·0) = (1/3)/(1/2) = 2/3 for the switch door.
hints: Numerator = prior of Door 2 × its likelihood = 1/3 · 1.
Denominator = total chance of the reveal = 1/3·½ + 1/3·1 + 1/3·0 = 1/2.

beats.prompt: Scale up to 100 doors: you pick 1, the host opens 98 goat doors and leaves one other door closed. What is P(win by switching)?

feedback.correct: Your door is 1/100; the host opens 98 goats, forced to leave exactly one other door closed → that door holds the other 99/100. Bigger N makes switching more compelling, not less.
hints: Your one pick is right 1/100 of the time — that never changes.
The host's near-total forcing funnels the remaining 99/100 onto the single door he leaves closed.
Switch wins 99/100.

beats.prompt: Three methods all converge on 2/3 — reveal each lens.

lenses.label: Enumerate
lenses.body: Car is equally likely behind 1, 2, 3; switching wins whenever you first picked a goat — 2 of 3 times

lenses.label: Odds
lenses.body: Your door 1/3 vs the rest 2/3; the host funnels the 2/3 onto one door → 2:1 → 2/3

feedback.correct: The likelihood table, the enumeration of car positions, and the odds funnel all give 2/3 — not a wording trick.
hints: Reveal each lens.
They are three roads to the same number.
All three say 2/3.

beats.prompt: Transfer: three closed boxes, exactly one holds a prize. You pick Box A. A friend who does NOT know where the prize is opens one of the other two boxes at random; it happens to be empty (Box C). What is P(prize is in Box B)?

interaction.scenario: Three boxes, one holds a prize. You pick Box A. A friend who does not know where the prize is opens one of the other two boxes uniformly at random; it turns out to be empty (Box C).

feedback.correct: Condition on the actual evidence — that the friend's random open revealed Box C empty — exactly as with the random-host Monty: \[ P(\text{prize at B}\mid\text{C empty}) = \frac{P(\text{prize at B})\,P(\text{C empty}\mid\text{prize at B})}{\sum_j P(\text{prize at }j)\,P(\text{C empty}\mid\text{prize at }j)} \] The friend picks uniformly from the two unchosen boxes, so the chance Box C ends up empty is: \(P(\text{C empty}\mid\text{prize at A}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\), \(P(\text{C empty}\mid\text{prize at B}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\), \(P(\text{C empty}\mid\text{prize at C}) = 0\). Each prior is \(\tfrac{1}{3}\). Substituting: \[ P(\text{prize at B}\mid\text{C empty}) = \frac{\tfrac{1}{3}\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}}{\tfrac{1}{3}\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}+\tfrac{1}{3}\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}+\tfrac{1}{3}\cdot 0} = \frac{1}{2} \] An uninformed opener carries no information about which live box hides the prize — switching and staying are equal at \(\tfrac{1}{2}\).
hints: The opener is uninformed, so condition on the data: given where the prize is, what is \(P(\text{C ends up empty})\)?

If the prize is at A or B, the friend reveals C empty with probability \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) each — likelihoods \([\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2},0]\). Equal likelihoods for the two live boxes → no update between them.
Posterior: \(\frac{\tfrac{1}{6}}{\tfrac{1}{6}+\tfrac{1}{6}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\) for each live box.

beats.prompt: Mastery challenge: same three doors, you pick Door 1 — but this host opens a remaining door completely at random and it happens to reveal a goat behind Door 3. What is P(win by switching)?

interaction.scenario: Same three doors, you pick Door 1 — but this host opens a remaining door completely at random (not forced to avoid the car) and it happens to reveal a goat behind Door 3.

fields.label: \(P(\text{switch wins})\) when host opened at random

feedback.correct: Apply the 3-hypothesis update with the random host's likelihoods: \[ P(\text{car at D}_i\mid\text{D3 open}) = \frac{P(\text{car at D}_i)\,P(\text{D3 open}\mid\text{car at D}_i)}{\sum_j P(\text{car at D}_j)\,P(\text{D3 open}\mid\text{car at D}_j)} \] The random host picks uniformly from the two remaining doors, so: \(P(\text{D3 open}\mid\text{car at D1}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\), \(P(\text{D3 open}\mid\text{car at D2}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\), \(P(\text{D3 open}\mid\text{car at D3}) = 0\). Each prior is \(\tfrac{1}{3}\). Substituting: \[ P(\text{car at D2}\mid\text{D3 open}) = \frac{\tfrac{1}{3}\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}}{\tfrac{1}{3}\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}+\tfrac{1}{3}\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}+\tfrac{1}{3}\cdot 0} = \frac{\tfrac{1}{6}}{\tfrac{1}{3}} = \frac{1}{2} \] Likelihoods \([\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2},0]\) are equal for both live doors — no update between them. It was never the goat; it was the forced choice that made switching \(\tfrac{2}{3}\).
hints: This host picks randomly, so ask: given where the car is, what is \(P(\text{D3 opened})\)?
If the car is at D1 or D2, he opens D3 with prob \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) each — equal likelihoods \([\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2},0]\). Equal likelihoods for the two live doors → no update between them.
Posterior: \(\frac{\tfrac{1}{6}}{\tfrac{1}{6}+\tfrac{1}{6}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\) for each live door.

beats.prompt: Reveal the recap to consolidate the host-as-evidence insight.

feedback.correct: The host's action is the evidence, and its likelihood comes from his rules. Run the 3-hypothesis update and the forcing pushes the switch door to 2/3.
hints: A standard host is forced (L=1) when the car is elsewhere, free (L=½) when it's yours — switch wins 2/3.
A random host who happens to reveal a goat gives no edge — 1/2. Intent, not the goat, is the clue.
Next up: the exact event you condition on is the whole ballgame.
